html:
Severfield PLC (SFRL.XC / LON:SFR)

Severfield is a messy but attractive turnaround: the UK’s dominant structural steel fabricator, priced like a permanently impaired contractor, while its next earnings cycle is being rebuilt around infrastructure, data centers, nuclear, offshore wind, battery plants, and a fast-growing India JV.

The old bullish draft was wrong on two important facts. Severfield is not net cash, and the consolidated UK and Europe order book is not £800m. The better thesis is more nuanced: at roughly 25.6p, or about £77m market cap, Severfield has expected FY26 pre-IFRS 16 net debt of about £28m, but also has a £438m UK and Europe order book, a £331m Indian JV order book, new management, a discontinued non-core modular business, and an equity-accounted India stake that may be worth a large fraction of the whole market cap.

The market is anchored on the pain: FY25 revenue of £450.9m, underlying PBT down to £18.1m, statutory losses from bridge remedial costs, suspended dividends, weak H1 FY26 profit, and cautious FY27 guidance of only £12m to £15m underlying PBT. That is why the stock is not obviously cheap on near-term numbers. At the midpoint of FY27 guidance, after tax EPS is only around 3.4p, so the shares trade near 7.5x to 8x forward earnings and roughly 4.5x FY27 EV/EBITDA. Cheap, but not insane.

The upside comes if FY27 is the trough-margin bridge year, not the new normal. Severfield historically earned far better margins than the current guide implies. FY24 underlying operating margin was 8.1%, FY25 was 4.8%, and Edison notes that 2015 to 2025 operating margins averaged about 6.9%. If management can get back to even 5% to 5.5% operating margins on £490m to £520m revenue, the business can earn roughly £23m to £26m PBT, or 5.5p to 6.5p EPS. At today’s price, that is 4x to 5x normalized earnings for the UK market leader in a steel-intensive infrastructure cycle.

The variant perception is that Severfield is not just a UK commercial construction supplier. The company is increasingly tied to sectors where the steel package is mission-critical and the customer cares more about execution certainty than saving a few basis points on contract price: AI data centers, grid and energy infrastructure, nuclear, offshore wind, battery manufacturing, transport upgrades, and defense facilities. The latest H1 report specifically flags AI-driven data center demand, energy security, transport, nuclear, and the UK government’s £725bn 10-year infrastructure strategy as medium-term drivers. The government’s AI Growth Zones and priority treatment for data-center power access strengthen the same demand backdrop.

The India JV is the most underappreciated asset. JSSL, Severfield’s 50% JV with JSW Steel, grew H1 FY26 revenue 34%, lifted operating margin to 7.1%, and had a record order book that later rose to £331m. The Gujarat expansion should lift in-house capacity from roughly 114k tonnes to 184k tonnes, with longer-term scope around 265k tonnes. The July 2025 option agreement with JSW implied up to £20m for 24.9% of JSSL, which triangulates Severfield’s 50% stake near £40m. Even if that option was primarily a liquidity backstop, it is a useful third-party value marker: the India stake alone may represent about half the current market cap.

The new management setup matters. CEO Paul McNerney joined from Laing O’Rourke in November 2025, CFO Andrew Page started in February 2026, and the first major action was to shut Severfield Modular Solutions, a sub-scale business that contributed £24.2m revenue and only £0.4m PBT in FY25, then lost money in H1 FY26. The June 23, 2026 FY26 results and strategic review are the immediate catalyst. If management lays out credible medium-term margin and ROCE targets, the market can stop valuing Severfield off depressed FY26 and FY27 guidance.

Retail sentiment is also useful. ADVFN discussion is cautious, focused on thin 2.5% to 3% margins, project delays, Middle East risk, bridge issues, and whether shareholders will forgive management. That is the point: expectations are low, the stock is illiquid, and the narrative is still “damaged contractor.” A credible margin path, evidence that bridge costs are contained, and continued India momentum can change that label quickly.

The main risks are real. Near-term work has been won at tighter margins. Several larger projects have shifted later into FY27. Debt is not trivial for a small-cap contractor. Bridge-related claims could linger. A severe UK or European construction downturn could leave factories underutilized. India is promising but capital intensive, and JV debt sits at the JV level. Severfield has also had enough historical operational disappointments that investors are rationally skeptical.

Still, the stock is priced for a business that earns 2% to 3% margins indefinitely. The base case only needs Severfield to become an average version of its former self, with a higher-quality order mix and a larger India option.

Using a current price of about 25.6p:

Bear case: 14p, down 45%. FY27 slips again, PBT falls below £10m, bridge or working-capital costs reappear, and the stock trades on depressed 2p EPS with no dividend support.

Base case: 52p, up 103%. FY28 EPS normalizes around 5.2p to 5.5p, the strategic review restores confidence, debt remains controlled, and the market gives Severfield a 9.5x to 10x multiple.

Bull case: 85p, up 232%. UK and Europe margins recover toward 6% to 7%, India becomes a visible £4m to £6m annual earnings contributor to Severfield, data-center and energy work tightens fabrication capacity, and the stock rerates to 12x roughly 7p EPS with renewed dividends or buybacks.
